coredata.dc:title: Size effect on oral absorption in polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ran, Fu
Lei, Wei
Cui, Yu
Jiao, Jian
Mao, Yuling
Wang, Shengyu
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: In this manuscript, the effect of the particle size of polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon (MPP) nanoparticles on enhancing oral absorption of a water-insoluble drug is first investigated. The insoluble drug, fenofibrate (Fen), was selected as the model drug loaded in the MPP nanoparticles. MPP nanoparticles with different particle sizes were designed for improving the oral bioavailability of drugs, in which the branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) and polyacrylic acid (PAA) were modified on the surfaces of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCNs) with amide bonds. In addition, PEI-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PCA) and radioisotope 125I were applied to label the MPP nanoparticles to trace in the vivo process. According to the data, the MPP nanoparticles could markedly improve the dissolution rate and oral bioavailability of Fen. Interestingly, the MPP nanoparticle size had a notable effect on Fen oral absorption, and intermediate sized MPP nanoparticles were expected to be more ideal oral drug carriers. The nanoparticles were safe and easily excreted. These findings present the prospect of MPP nanoparticles for oral application, and guides the rational design of an oral delivery system with respect to particle size.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous carbon
Nanoparticle
Size
Polymer
Oral bioavailability

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading process and drug content analysis
In vitro drug release behaviors and in vivo pharmacokinetics study
125I labeling and in vivo bio-distribution of M2PP nanoparticles
Preparation and characterization of MPP nanoparticles
Drug content analysis and solid-state characterization of drug loaded nanoparticles
In vitro drug release behaviors and in vivo pharmacokinetics study of Fen loaded MPP nanoparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81273449 ). Disclosures We have no conflicts of interest. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of MCN with different average sizes. (A) 100nm (HRTEM), (B) 100nm, (C) and (D) 250nm, (E) and (F) 450nm.
Characterization of MCN. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of MCN.
Characterization of MPP nanoparticles. The FT-IR spectra (A) and zeta potential changes (B) of MCNs, MCN-COOH, MCN@PEI and MPP nanoparticles (n=3). The TEM of M1PP (C), M2PP (D) and M3PP (E).

Characterization of PCA. (A) TEM image of PCA and (B) HRTEM image. (C) FT-IR spectra of the PCA. (D) XRD pattern of the PCA. (E) The emission spectra of PCA obtained at the excitation wavelength of 300–480nm with a 20-nm increase. (F) UV–Vis absorption spectra, and FL spectra of the PCA.
Solid state characterization. DSC curves (A) and XRD patterns (B) of Fen, MPP nanoparticles and Fen-loaded MPP nanoparticles.
In vitro drug release behaviors. Dissolution profiles of Fen, Fen-M1PP, Fen-M2PP, Fen-M3PP and commercial capsule in 0.3% SDS solution (n=3).
In vivo pharmacokinetics study. Plasma concentration–time profiles of Fen commercial capsules and the drug loaded MPP nanoparticles in SD rats (n=5).
Biodistribution in major organs. Kunming mice sacrificed at 2h, 6h, 10h, 12h after oral administration of PCA-M2PP.
In vivo bio-distribution. The bio-distribution of M2PP in mice at different time points after oral administration (n=3).
Schematic illustration of (A) synthetic route of MCNs with different particle sizes; (B) synthetic route of MPP, and (C) two kinds of in vivo tracing strategies.

head.ce:title: Size effect on oral absorption in polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutics, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this manuscript, the effect of the particle size of polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon (MPP) nanoparticles on enhancing oral absorption of a water-insoluble drug is first investigated. The insoluble drug, fenofibrate (Fen), was selected as the model drug loaded in the MPP nanoparticles. MPP nanoparticles with different particle sizes were designed for improving the oral bioavailability of drugs, in which the branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) and polyacrylic acid (PAA) were modified on the surfaces of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCNs) with amide bonds. In addition, PEI-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PCA) and radioisotope 125I were applied to label the MPP nanoparticles to trace in the vivo process. According to the data, the MPP nanoparticles could markedly improve the dissolution rate and oral bioavailability of Fen. Interestingly, the MPP nanoparticle size had a notable effect on Fen oral absorption, and intermediate sized MPP nanoparticles were expected to be more ideal oral drug carriers. The nanoparticles were safe and easily excreted. These findings present the prospect of MPP nanoparticles for oral application, and guides the rational design of an oral delivery system with respect to particle size.

ce:section.ce:para: Physicochemical properties, such as size, shape, surface chemistry and structure, play important roles in determining nanoparticle behaviors in vitro and in vivo [1,2]. For an oral delivery system, particle size is a key factor, which ultimately affects the gastrointestinal uptake, in vitro digestion and drug release from nanoparticles [3,4]. Although there are many reports on the oral absorption effects of particle size, these researches were mainly focused on polymeric nanoparticles [5]. Researchers seldom paid attention to the effect that the particle size of inorganic nanoparticles had on an oral drug delivery system, especially mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCNs) [6]. Compared with traditional lipid-based nanoparticles or polymeric nanoparticles, MCNs exhibit prominent characteristics, such as a large surface area, controllable shape, favorable biocompatibility, good stability, and especially their easily functionalized surface, making them ideal candidates for drug delivery [7,8]. Additionally, some studies confirmed that MCNs could effectively improve the oral absorption of poorly water-soluble drugs [9,10].

However, the effect of particle size on oral drug delivery has rarely been studied due to the difficulties in the preparation of size-controlled MCNs. In addition, π-π stacking interactions between the fluorescent dyes and the walls of carbon spheres could make the fluorescence quench through photoinduced electron transfer, which makes it difficult to trace the MCNs’ in vivo process [11,12]. Therefore, a novel and multifunctional drug delivery system should be established to solve these problems. According to some reports, advantages of the surface functionalized nanoparticles with hydrophilic polymers are as follows: (1) improved dispersibility and stability, such as polyethyleneimine (PEI) [13,14], polyacrylic acid (PAA) [15,16]; (2) enhanced mucoadhesion and intestinal uptake, such as poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA); (3) reduced the toxicity of nanoparticles; and (4) decreased π-π stacking interaction [17–20]. Thus, it is extremely important to select appropriate polymers.

Recently, compared with commercial fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted the attention of researchers as being one of the most popular photoluminescent materials due to their low toxicity and environmental friendliness. Additionally, it has been confirmed that surface passivation was critical to improve the fluorescent efficiency of CQDs, and the surface groups of passivating agents could connect with a suitable drug delivery to play an important role in tracing [21,22].

In this work, novel polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon nanoparticles of various sizes were developed to investigate their effects on improving the oral absorption of poorly water-soluble drugs. For the first time, MCNs with tunable particle sizes were synthesized by adjusting volume of ethanol during preparation. PEI and PAA grafted on the surface of MCNs were applied as the biocompatible materials as well as barriers to decrease π-π stacking interaction between MCNs and CQDs, which made CQDs trace MCNs in vivo. Interestingly, the in vivo process of nanoparticles was observed with two methods as follows: one method involves coating with PEI-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PCA), which uses citric acid (CA) as a carbon source and PEI as surface passivating agents, and the other method was associated with the radioactive isotope 125I, as shown in Scheme 1 . To prevent the interference of surface charges in the drug loading process, fenofibrate, a biopharmaceutics classification system II (BCS II) and a neutral drug, was selected as a model drug. In addition, the in vitro dissolution behavior, in vivo pharmacokinetics, and gastrointestinal mucosa irritation were also discussed.

Formaldehyde solution, resorcinol, citric acid (CA), ammonium persulfate, sulfuric acid (98%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98%), and hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC, >99%) were obtained from Shan Dong Yu Wang Reagent Company (China). Poly (acrylic acid) (PAA, MW =2000Da), polyethyleneimine (Branched PEI, MW =25kDa), p-aminophenol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade and were used without further purification.

According to the preceding method, MCNs with tunable particle sizes were prepared by altering the amount of absolute ethanol [23]. First, CTAC (1.04g, 25wt% in water) and ammonia (0.1ml) were added to the solution containing deionized water (H2O, 19ml) and anhydrous alcohol (EtOH, 4–8ml) with stirring at room temperature. After 30min, resorcinol (0.2g) was dissolved in the aforementioned solution and continually stirred for 30min. Subsequently, TEOS (0.36ml) and formaldehyde solution (0.28ml) were introduced dropwise to the reaction solution with intensive stirring, and the system continued stirring at 30°C for 24h. The as-prepared products were purified by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, and dried under vacuum. Afterwards, samples were calcined at 700°C and kept for 3h for carbonization. Finally, these CSi samples were immersed in 10% hydrofluoric acid for 24h to remove silica to obtain the MCNs. For further functionalization, the carboxyl groups were modified on the surface of MCNs via a wet oxidation process [24]. A total of 200mg MCN was added to the solution containing ammonium persulfate (1.36g), sulfuric acid (0.639ml, 98wt%) and H2O (12ml), then stirring was continued at 60°C for 3h. In turn, 30mg MCN-COOH was uniformly dispersed into the solution containing 30ml of PBS (0.1M, pH 5.8), 30mg of EDC and 9mg of NHS under stirring for 1h to activate carboxyl groups. Subsequently, 10ml of PEI solution (3mg/ml) was introduced dropwise to the above solution, and the system continued stirring at room temperature for 12h. Finally, the resulting solution was centrifuged to obtain MCN@PEI, which was then washed several times with deionized water to remove excess PEI and dried under vacuum. In addition, a similar procedure was adapted in the process of grafting PAA on the surface of MCN@PEI to prepare MPP nanoparticles.

PEI (0.1g) and CA (0.4g) were uniformly dissolved in deionized water (20ml); then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160°C for 6h. Afterwards, the dark brown solution was centrifuged at 15000rpm to obtain a bright yellow solution that was then dialyzed against deionized water (MWCO 3500Da) for 24h and replaced with fresh water every 2h. Subsequently, the final brown powder was obtained by freeze-drying technology. MPP nanoparticles (5mg) were mixed with PCA (10mg/mL) in deionized water and stirred for 1h at room temperature, then washed three times with deionized water. Finally, the PCA was attached to the surface of MPP nanoparticles as a fluorescent probe for imaging by an electrostatic interaction.

Quantum yields were determined based on the experimental procedures described by Kundu et al. A quinine sulfate standard (QY=0.54 at 360nm) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4, and the PCA was dissolved in deionized water. The QY of PCA was calculated by the following Eq. (1) [25].
(1)
QY
u
=
QY
s
[
( I / A )
u
×
( A / I )
s
]
(
n
u
/
n
s
)
2
where “A” is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index of the solvent (1.34 for 0.1M H2SO4, and 1.396 for water), “u” refers to the sample, and “s” indicates to the reference solution of quinine sulfate. The absorbance of all samples should be kept blow 0.10.

Nanoparticles were dispersed in deionized water with 0.3% SDS (w/v) to measure the hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potential by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The morphology and mesoporous nanostructure of MCNs and PCA were observed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Netherlands). The surface area and pore size distribution were detected via an adsorption analyzer (V-Sorb 2800P, China). The crystallinity of PCA and drug-loaded nanoparticles were determined by powder X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (Rigaku Geigerflex, Japan) with Cu Katarget (λ=1.5405Å, 30kV, 30mA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (MettlerToledo, Switzerland). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrum of PCA were evaluated by UV–visible spectrophotometry (UV-2000, USA) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4500, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry (FT-IR) spectra with a range from 500 to 4000cm−1 was determined on a Bruker IFS 55 (Switzerland) by KBr as a sample matrix.

Caco-2 cells were selected to evaluate the cellular toxicity of MPP nanoparticles, which were incubated in high glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% non-essential amino-acid and 1% antibiotics (penicillin/streptomycin) at 37°C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The in vitro cellular toxicity of the MPP in the Caco-2 cells was analyzed by MTT assay [26,27]. Briefly, Caco-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5×104 cells/well and incubated for 24h. The equal volume of fresh medium containing serial concentrations (50, 250, 500, 1000μg/mL) of nanoparticles (sterilized by ultraviolet lamp) was added after removing the culture medium. After 24h incubation, the cells were rinsed with Hanks’ balanced salt solution (HBSS, pH 7.4) three times to remove particle suspensions or blank culture medium. Then, 20μL MTT (final concentration 0.5mg/mL) was added and maintained for another 6h to quantify the living cells. The MTT medium was taken away, and the precipitates were dissolved in 200μL of DMSO. Lastly, the absorbance of each well was read at 540nm on a microplate reader (Bio-Rad, 680, America) and the percent viability was calculated as the ratio of treated cells over the negative control cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading process and drug content analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Prior to polymer capping, Fen (100mg) and MCNs (100mg) were dissolved in methanol in a sealed glass container, sonicated for 30min, and mildly stirred for 12h at room temperature for drug adsorption. After air drying at 25°C to volatilize solvent with a gentle stirring rate, drug loading was determined by extracting some Fen-loaded MCNs. Then, Fen-MCNs were rinsed by the solvent of ethanol/water volume at a ratio of 1:9 to remove the surface adsorbed Fen and then dried under vacuum. Subsequently, drug-loaded particles were redispersed in PBS (0.1M, pH 5.8) and covalent connectivity of PEI and PAA was executed. To ensure the drug was released from the mesoporous pore channel of MCN rather than the polymers in the following experiments, the above samples were washed again to remove the adsorbed drug on the polymer surfaces. The drug content (%) was calculated according to the following Eq. (2):
(2)
D ( % ) =
M
1
M
× 100
where “D” is the drug loading, “M1” is the weight of drug in samples, “M” is the weight of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release behaviors and in vivo pharmacokinetics study
ce:section.ce:para: Based on USPⅡ, the release studies of Fen from MPP nanoparticles were conducted using paddle method with a dissolution apparatus (ZRS-8GD, Tianjin TDTF Technology Co., Ltd.). Dissolution tests were implemented in triplicate in deionized water with 0.3% SDS (w/v) under a constant temperature (37°C) at a paddle speed of 100rpm. At certain time intervals, 5-ml samples with the released Fen content were separated from the release medium and analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 290nm. At the same time, an equal amount of fresh medium was supplied to keep the volume constant.

To study the distinction on the oral absorption of Fen-loaded MPP nanoparticles with different particle sizes, twenty male SD rats (250±20g) were randomly divided into 4 groups, with 5 rats per group. Before the test, the rats were fasted overnight. The content of commercial fenofibrate capsules, Fen-M1PP, Fen-M2PP and Fen-M3PP was intragastrically administered to rats at a dose of Fen at 10mg/kg. Then, 0.5-ml blood samples were collected from the eye socket vein at 0.5h, 1h, 3h, 4h, 6h, 8h, 9h, 10h, 12h, 24h and 36h after dosing and then centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min, and the plasma samples were extracted and then analyzed by HPLC (Hitachi. Japan). The Fen pharmacokinetic parameters were calculated using DAS 2.1 version (Boying Corporation, China). In addition, the relative bioavailability was calculated by the following Eq. (3).
(3)
Rel bioavailability =
(
AUC
0 - ∞
)
a
/
(
AUC
0 - ∞
)
b
× 100 %
where “AUC” is the area under the plasma concentration versus time curve, “a” refers to the fenofibrate-loaded MPP nanoparticles, and “b” indicates to the commercial capsules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 125I labeling and in vivo bio-distribution of M2PP nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 5 Kunming mice (30–35g) were used to investigate the bio-distribution of M2PP nanoparticles by a Carestream MS FXPRO (Bruker/Carestream, Rochester, NY). The control group was given 0.5ml of physiological saline while the other groups were administered a suspension of 0.5ml of PCA-M2PP (5mg/ml). Five mice were sacrificed and imaged at 2, 6, 10 and 12h after oral administration for ex vivo analysis.

M2PP (mid-sized) nanoparticles were chosen as an example to explore the distribution of MPP nanoparticles in vivo. Initially, p-aminophenol was covalently linked with the nanoparticles by amide bond, then the synthesized products were labeled with 125I via the following method. Simply, 1.23mg nanoparticles (15mg/ml) and 1.76mCi 125I were added to the PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4) under stirring for 5min at room temperature. Then, the labeled carbon nanoparticles were purified by repeated centrifugation and washed to remove unreacted 125I. A radiolabeling yield of 38% was achieved. Kunming mice weighing approximately 20g were divided into six groups. The first group was given physiological saline as control, and the other five groups were intragastrically administered 125I labeled M2PP nanoparticles with a dosage of 50mg/kg, the blood was collected at 2h, 12h, 24h, 48h and 7d post-administration. Subsequently, the animals were sacrificed and the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, stomach and intestines were isolated. At the same time, to prevent interference on the determination of organ radioactivity, the stomach and intestine needed to be cleaned to remove their contents. Finally, the radioactivity of the blood and tissue samples were measured by a gamma counter (Applied Physics Institute of China in Rihuan Co., Ltd.), which showed that the% oral dose per gram blood (%ID/g) for all measured samples.

SD rats (weighing approximately 200g each) were divided into four groups. The control group was treated with physiological saline, and the other three groups were given M1PP, M2PP and M3PP nanoparticles suspension with dosages of 100mg/kg. Five hours after administration, all rats were sacrificed and their stomach and duodenum tissues were collected. Subsequently, the samples were rinsed with normal saline repeatedly, then fixed in a 10% (v/v) formaldehyde solution and embedded in paraffin for consecutive disposing the tissues into 5μm-thickness sections. Finally, the processed tissues were observed by a stereomicroscope (Olympus, CX41, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MPP nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
The preparation process of MCNs with polymers serving as biocompatible materials and barriers to decrease π-π stacking interaction between MCNs and CQDs connected by amide bond is described in Scheme 1. As shown in Fig. 1 , monodispersed and spherical carbon nanoparticles were prepared, which all had a highly ordered mesoporous structure with a narrow, worm-like pore channel. Moreover, nanoparticles with the smallest size (approximately 100nm) (MCN1) were obtained under conditions of 4ml of ethanol. By increasing the volume of ethanol to 6ml and 8ml, the average diameters of MCNs orderly increased to 250nm (MCN2) and 450nm (MCN3), respectively. After polymers were grafted on MCNs, MPP nanoparticles were denoted as M1PP, M2PP and M3PP with the increase of particle size. As shown in Fig. 3
(C–E), the polymers grafted on the surface of MCNs could not be observed by TEM. The hydrodynamic sizes of all nanoparticles are shown in Supplementary Table S1. After the polymer coating process, the hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles indicated a significant increase, which may decrease π-π stacking interaction between carbon nanoparticles and quantum dots so that the PCA became a better tracer. Meanwhile, as shown in Supplementary Table S1, the PDI data were all less than 0.2, indicating that the increase in particle size was due to the successful grafting of the polymer onto the surface of the MCNs, rather than the aggregation of the particles during the grafting of the polymer. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of MCNs are presented in Fig. 2. The MCN samples exhibited typical type-IV hysteresis, indicative of the presence of mesopores. The nanoparticle parameters are also summarized in Supplementary Table S1. After being covered by polymers, the surface area of nanoparticles rapidly declined to 30m2/g, the pore volume and pore size became undetectable, which demonstrated that the pore tunnels were blocked by polymers. Hence, the drug loading process should be conducted prior to the polymer covering process.

Infrared spectrum and zeta potential of nanoparticles with different modifications confirmed the successful fabrication of MPP nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 3A, compared with the IR peaks of MCNs, a new peak at 1715cm−1 appears after the oxidation process that belongs to the carbonyl stretching vibration peak, indicating the existence of carboxylic groups. After polymers were grafted on MCN-COOH, a wide peak emerged at 1631cm−1 due to the formation of amide bonds between the MCN-COOH, PEI and PAA. Meanwhile, the appearance of a strong absorption peak at 2800–3000cm−1 should be attributed to CH stretching, indicating the successful grafting of polymers. As shown in Fig. 3B, MCNs had a zeta potential value of +12mV but decreased to −38mV after carboxylation, which also demonstrated the generation of the carboxyl groups. After MCN-COOH coated with PEI, the zeta potential was reversed from −38mV to +40mV, which was derived from the amino groups on the surface of PEI. After PAA coating on the nanoparticles, the zeta potential was inverted again from a positive value of +40mV to a negative value of −35mV, indicating the synthesis of MPP nanoparticles.

In this study, PCA was prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. The temperature and time were essential for the synthesis of high intensity fluorescent quantum dots. To avoid a large amount of PEI degeneration, the experiment was performed at 160°C for 6h. As shown from the inset of Fig. 4 F, the PCA solution showed bright blue fluorescence under excitation at 360nm. TEM images (Fig. 4A) exhibited that as-prepared PCA was well dispersed, and the spherical nanocrystals had a diameter of approximately 3.7nm (Fig. 4B). According to Fig. 4C, there are many characteristic peaks of PEI as follow: stretching vibrations of NH at 3444cm−1, stretching vibrations of CN at 1114cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1560cm−1, and the vibrational absorption peak of CO at 1650cm−1. It was demonstrated that most of these PEI chains were not carbonized during the thermal decomposition, which was advantageous for connection with MPP nanoparticles. Furthermore, the presence of the characteristic bonds of amide linkage (CONH) indicated that PEI had been tightly linked with the quantum dots formed by CA. The PCA XRD pattern displayed a characteristic peak centered at 24° (Fig. 4D), which was attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon materials. The QY (excited at 360nm) was measured to be ca. 33.1% using quinine sulfate as a reference, which was appropriate for bioimaging applications [28,29]. PCA possessed the maximum excitation and emission peak at 360nm and 460nm (Fig. 4F). Simultaneously, PCA exhibited an excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 4E), which could contribute to the surface state and particle size of the carbon quantum dot [30].

As shown in Fig. S2, there were no significant changes in cells exposed to nanoparticles compared with control cells, which indicated that the MPP synthesized in this study was nontoxic or had a very low toxicity. Concurrently, as the dose increased, the cell viability remained nearly constant, even at a concentration of 1000μg/ml. The good compatibility could be due to several factors, including their stable physicochemical properties and polymer functionalization. In addition, the three different sizes of MPP exhibited no significant differences in Caco-2 cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug content analysis and solid-state characterization of drug loaded nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: During the Fen-MPP nanoparticles preparation process, the systems were rinsed twice by a solvent of ethanol/water (1:9, v/v) to remove the surface-adsorbed Fen. First, the drugs absorbed on the surface of MCNs needed to be rinsed after loading. Subsequently, to ensure the drug was released from the mesoporous pore channel of MCNs rather than the polymers, MPP nanoparticles were rinsed again after grafting polymers. In this work, we aimed to investigate the effect of polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon nanoparticles with different particle sizes on improving the oral absorption of poorly water-soluble drugs. To reduce the effect of different drug loading resulting in different oral bioavailabilities, the drug loading of the three systems can be made approximately equal by controlling the amount of the rinse solution during the two carrier flushings. The drug content of Fen-loaded nanoparticles was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 290nm. The results showed that the loaded amounts of Fen in MCNs with different particle sizes were 49.0%, 47.4% and 47.3%. After the polymer coating and washing process, some drug was lost, and the drug loading decreased to 27.1%, 25.1% and 24.9%. The solid state of pure Fen, pure carriers and Fen-loaded nanoparticles were assessed by DSC and XRD. A crystalline drug shows a single depression at its melting point in its DSC curve. If the drug exists in a non-crystalline form, no depression is observed. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the DSC curve of pure Fen showed an intense endothermic peak at the melting point (81°C), but no signs of pure carrier and Fen-MPP nanoparticles appeared. It was demonstrated that the drug in non-crystalline was in the pore channels of nanoparticles. To further confirm the crystal property of Fen, XRD was also used to evaluate the crystallinity of the drug, which is shown in Fig. 5B. XRD patterns of pure Fen indicated several sharp and typical diffraction peaks, and after being loaded into MPP nanoparticles, the diffraction peaks could not be detected. This phenomenon illustrated that the narrow pore channel suppressed Fen crystallization, which could also explain the increased dissolution rate and oral bioavailability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release behaviors and in vivo pharmacokinetics study of Fen loaded MPP nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the effect of particle sizes on Fen release behavior, we compared the Fen release profiles of Fen-loaded M1PP, M2PP, M3PP, raw drug powder as well as its commercial capsules. As shown in Fig. 6 , the cumulative release of the raw Fen was only 35% in 0.3% SDS dissolution medium due to its water-insolubility, showing the worst dissolution rate. In comparison, Fen-loaded MPP nanoparticles exhibited faster drug release compared with the raw Fen, which showed that MPP nanoparticles had the potential for improving Fen oral bioavailability. Specifically, the release behavior of Fen-loaded M2PP nanoparticles improved markedly, and the cumulative release of Fen reached up to 80% within 1h. In contrast, the accumulation release of Fen-M1PP was no more than 72%, which was slower than other Fen-MPP nanoparticles. The accumulation release of Fen-MPP followed the order of Fen-M2PP>Fen-M3PP>Fen-M1PP. Interestingly, the release of drugs was a diffusion-controlled process, which would be greatly affected by the external surface area and channel length of the carriers. Thus, the nanoparticles with small sizes rather than larger sizes should display the fastest release, according to the theory of diffusion. The reason that MPP nanoparticles with an intermediate size possessed the best release behavior compared with other particles might be that the polymers grafted on the MCNs blocked the surface pores of MCNs and influenced the porosity and surface properties of carriers, which subsequently resulted in a strong impact on the in vitro behavior of the carriers. In addition, because fenofibrate would be metabolized to fenofibric acid in plasma, the in vivo pharmacokinetics study was implemented by determining the content of fenofibric acid. The plasma concentration-time curve of fenofibric acid after a single oral administration with a dose of Fen at 10mg/kg is presented in Fig. 7 , and the pharmacokinetic parameters are shown in Supplementary Table S2. Fen-loaded M2PP nanoparticles exhibited a higher Cmax and better improvement of oral bioavailability, and AUC0-36
h of fenofibric acid also followed the order of Fen-M2PP>Fen-M3PP>Fen-M1PP, which was consistent with the results of the in vitro drug release test. In comparison with commercial capsules, the AUC0-36
h of Fen-M2PP was enhanced over 1.5-fold, which could be attributed to the improved solubility and faster dissolution rate of Fen from Fen-M2PP nanoparticles. In contrast, as a result of the release profiles of Fen-M1PP, the AUC0-36
h of Fen-M1PP was lower than the commercial formula.

After polymers coating process, the obtained MPP nanoparticles could improve the oral absorption of Fen, which may be caused by the following factors: (1) Fen was highly dispersed in the pore channels of nanoparticles, and the crystallite size of Fen was reduced to nano-sized range; (2) Owing to the presence of Fen in a non-crystalline form, the intermolecular binding energy decreased and Fen dissolution rate increased; and (3) Hydrophilic polymers (PEI and PAA) grafted on the surface of MCNs improved the adherence of MPP nanoparticles to intestinal mucosa and permeability across the intestinal barrier. All of these factors may lead to a rapid release of loaded Fen in the dissolution medium and the improvement of oral bioavailability. Meanwhile, the particle size had a notable effect on the oral drug delivery system, and MPP nanoparticles with an intermediate size exhibited the most efficient improvement on oral absorption of water-insoluble drugs. Polymer nanoparticles with a small particle size could be absorbed faster through the gastrointestinal tract into the blood circulation, which might be attributed to the higher permeability across the intestinal barrier and higher uptake efficiency in intestinal epithelial cells. Conversely this result might be partially attributed to the fact that smaller nanoparticles improve their adherence to intestinal mucosa because of the relative small mass facilitating their penetration into the mucus layer. However, compared with the polymer nanoparticles, inorganic nanocarbons are prone to aggregation. Moreover, the smaller the particle size, the worse the dispersibility, which will result in the poor oral bioavailability of drugs, as well as a change in behavior of carrier in vivo. Therefore, the phenomenon that the MPP with intermediate sizes yield the best Fen oral absorption may be the result of the synergistic effect of carrier dispersibility and particle size. Based on the above results, we hypothesized that M2PP (250nm) nanoparticles may be the optimum size for oral absorption of water insoluble drug in the gastrointestinal tract among the three carriers. Ye et al. also found glucose-based mesoporous carbon nanoparticles approximately 230nm in particle size could enhance the oral bioavailability of poorly bioavailable raloxifene [31]. R.J. Ahern et al. investigated the effect of different drug loading methods of mesoporous silica (SBA-15) on improving the in vitro dissolution of Fen, with the best dissolution rate of 75%, which is less than that of Fen-M2PP (80%) [32]. S.M. Van et al. researched the impact of different pore sizes of mesoporous silica on improving the oral bioavailability of Fen. The bioavailability of Fen was 1.3–1.4 times higher than that of the commercial preparations, which was less than 1.5-fold of Fen-M2PP [33]. In addition, K. Bukara and J.P. O'Shea improved the oral bioavailability of Fen by using mesoporous silicon, and the bioavailability was significantly higher than that of the raw drugs. However, the dissolution rate was slower than that of Fen-M2PP, and M2PP was easier to excrete, indicating that M2PP nanoparticles had a potential to improve the oral bioavailability of drugs as a safer carrier [34,35].

Bio-distribution, as a critical factor for evaluating the safety of novel nanomaterials, needs to be studied [36]. First, we detected the PCA-M2PP bio-distribution in the major organs of Kunming mice after oral administration. These organs were removed according to settled schedule and imaged for ex vivo analysis [37]. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 , M2PP nanoparticles were mainly distributed in the duodenum, jejunum and ileum at 2h. While at 6h, the fluorescence signal could only be seen in the jejunum and ileum. As time increases, the fluorescence decreased gradually. At 12h, almost no fluorescence signal was observed. Simultaneously, the fluorescence signal was barely found in other organs throughout the process, which may be due to the minimal intestinal uptake or rapid excretion of nanoparticles. To verify the above conjecture and study the distribution in other organs, isotopic labeling with high sensitivity and specificity was chosen [38,39]. According to the results of in vivo imaging, 2h and 12h were selected as the first two time points. As shown in Fig. 9 , at 2h post injection, the carrier had already entered the blood, and a high level of radioactivity was distributed in the stomach and other major organs. After 12h, 125I radioactivity levels rapidly decreased, and it was difficult to detect radioactivity after 24h after oral administration. Meanwhile, it was found that 125I labeled M2PP nanoparticles showed no obvious tissue uptake after oral administration, which may indicate the rather limited intestinal adsorption and rapid excretion of nanoparticles. The previous reports also supported the results on functionalized nano-graphene oxide [40].

It is essential for oral drug delivery systems to carry out the gastrointestinal mucosa irritation experiment. To evaluate the safety of MPP nanoparticles for oral delivery system, histological evaluation was investigated. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S1, after administration of three different sized MPP nanoparticles at an overdose of 100mg/kg, there were no lesions or hyperemia phenomena in gastric and intestinal mucosa compared with the saline group, which further confirmed the safety of nanoparticles as oral drug carriers. Other studies also reported spherical nanocarbon materials had low toxicity and were biocompatible and non-pathogenic [41,42].

Mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCNs) have been widely studied in drug delivery due to their large surface area, controllable shape, and surface functionalization [7,8]. However, previous researchers seldom focused on the particle size of inorganic nanoparticles and their profound effect on oral drug delivery system, especially the mesoporous carbon nanoparticles [6]. In the present work, polymer-functionalized mesoporous carbon nanoparticles with different particle sizes were designed for improving the oral bioavailability of drugs in which the PEI and PAA were modified on the surfaces of MCN by amide bond. For the first time, the polymers were used not only as the biocompatible materials to improve the drugs oral bioavailability and biocompatibility [13–16], but also as barriers to decrease π-π stacking interaction between MCNs and CQDs. The in vitro drug release and in vivo pharmacokinetics results indicated that the MPP nanoparticle size had a notable effect on oral absorption, and the MPP nanoparticles with an intermediate size (250nm) are expected to be an ideal oral drug carrier for poorly water-soluble drugs. The polymers grafted on the surfaces of MCNs could markedly improve MCNs biocompatibility, and the MPP nanoparticles had properties of low toxicity and easy excretion. The present work provides direction for a reasonable design of an inorganic nanoparticle-based oral delivery system in terms of particle size. However, there are few reports on the oral absorption mechanism and in vivo process of MCNs; hence, the relevant cell and in vivo studies should be carried out in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81273449).
We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.09.088.
